actors.name: Externally Owned Account (EOA)
actors.description: An external user controlling an Ethereum account via a private key. Initiates transactions, signs them with secp256k1, and submits them to the network. The EOA is the originating trust boundary for all state mutations.

actors.name: Smart Contract
actors.description: On-chain bytecode deployed at an address. Contracts execute within the EVM and interact with state through opcodes. They can read/write storage, transfer ether, create other contracts, and self-destruct. Under EIP-7702, EOAs can delegate execution to contract code via SetCodeTransaction authorizations.

actors.name: Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)
actors.description: The deterministic execution environment that processes transactions. It manages the call stack, enforces gas limits, executes opcodes, and mediates all state access. The EVM is the trusted arbiter between external inputs and state modifications.

actors.name: State Manager
actors.description: The internal subsystem (state.py) managing the world state trie, account storage tries, transient storage, and snapshot-based transaction nesting. Provides CRUD operations on accounts and storage with atomicity guarantees through begin/commit/rollback transaction lifecycle.

actors.name: Transaction Processor
actors.description: The internal subsystem (transactions.py) responsible for encoding, decoding, validating, and authenticating transactions. Enforces gas limits, nonce constraints, initcode size limits, and performs cryptographic sender recovery from signatures.

actors.name: Validator / Block Builder
actors.description: The node operator that selects, orders, and includes transactions in blocks. Validators determine transaction ordering which affects MEV and can censor transactions. They invoke the transaction processor and EVM to execute transactions against state.

actors.name: Peer-to-Peer Network
actors.description: The gossip network layer through which transactions propagate from EOAs to validators. Transactions may be observed, reordered, or dropped by network participants including relayers and mempool operators.

actors.name: Trie Data Structure
actors.description: The Merkle Patricia Trie implementation used for both main account state and per-account storage. Provides cryptographic commitments (roots) that enable state verification. Used by the State Manager for all persistent data operations.

trust_boundaries.description: EOA submits a signed transaction to the transaction processor. The transaction crosses from an untrusted external domain into the validation pipeline. The transaction processor must verify the signature, validate gas limits, nonce, and structural constraints before any state mutation occurs.

After validation, the transaction processor hands a validated transaction to the EVM for execution. The EVM trusts that the transaction has been authenticated (sender recovered) and that intrinsic gas and structural checks have passed. The EVM enforces runtime gas metering and opcode-level constraints.

The EVM issues state mutation commands (get_account, set_storage, move_ether, etc.) to the State Manager. The State Manager trusts that the EVM has enforced access control, gas payment, and execution validity. The State Manager provides atomicity through snapshot-based transactions.

The State Manager delegates all persistent reads and writes to the Trie subsystem via trie_get/trie_set/copy_trie/root operations. The trie is trusted to provide correct cryptographic commitments (Merkle roots) and deterministic behavior.

Smart contracts execute opcodes that request storage reads/writes (SLOAD/SSTORE), balance transfers (CALL), contract creation (CREATE/CREATE2), and self-destruction (SELFDESTRUCT). The EVM mediates these requests, enforcing gas costs and preventing unauthorized cross-contract state access.

EOA broadcasts a signed transaction to the P2P network. The transaction is visible to all network participants during propagation. The network is untrusted and may leak, delay, reorder, or censor transactions.
Validators receive transactions from the P2P mempool. Validators select which transactions to include and in what order. This boundary is where MEV extraction and transaction censorship can occur.

The validator feeds transactions to the transaction processor for validation and execution. The validator controls transaction ordering but must submit syntactically valid transactions that pass the validation pipeline.

Under EIP-7702 (SetCodeTransaction), an EOA signs an authorization delegating code execution to a smart contract address. This creates a trust boundary where the EOA trusts the delegated contract code to act on its behalf, effectively blurring the EOA/contract boundary.

Smart contracts interact with transient storage (EIP-1153 TSTORE/TLOAD) and persistent storage (SSTORE/SLOAD) through the EVM, which delegates to the State Manager. Transient storage is scoped to a single transaction and follows the same snapshot/rollback lifecycle as persistent state.

Internal trust boundary within the State Manager between the snapshot/rollback mechanism and live state. The begin_transaction/commit_transaction/rollback_transaction lifecycle creates nested trust contexts where mutations can be atomically reverted. The snapshot mechanism must produce deep copies to prevent cross-context contamination.

assumptions.text: The secp256k1 elliptic curve cryptography is secure: signature recovery (recover_sender) correctly identifies the unique signer of a transaction, and no entity can forge a valid signature without the private key.
The keccak256 hash function is collision-resistant: signing hashes, state roots, storage roots, and address derivation all rely on keccak256 producing unique digests for distinct inputs.
The RLP encoding is deterministic and injective: encode_transaction and decode_transaction are inverse operations, and distinct transactions always produce distinct encodings.

The Merkle Patricia Trie implementation is correct: trie_get, trie_set, copy_trie, and root operations faithfully represent the authenticated data structure, and the root hash uniquely commits to the trie contents.

Snapshot deep copies in begin_transaction are truly independent: mutations to the live state after snapshotting do not affect the snapshot data, ensuring rollback_transaction can restore the exact pre-transaction state.

The EVM correctly enforces gas metering: after validate_transaction confirms sufficient gas (including EIP-7623 calldata floor and EIP-7825 TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT), the EVM will not permit execution beyond the gas budget.

Transaction nonces are correctly managed: increment_nonce guarantees monotonically increasing nonces per account (INV-034/035), and validate_transaction enforces the U64.MAX_VALUE upper bound (EIP-2681), preventing replay attacks.

Ether conservation is maintained: move_ether (INV-030) ensures the sender's balance decrease exactly equals the recipient's balance increase, and insufficient balance causes an assertion failure before any state change.

Account lifecycle rules are correctly enforced: empty accounts (zero nonce, empty code, zero balance) are automatically destroyed by modify_state (INV-028), and account_exists vs is_account_alive correctly distinguish between the empty account sentinel and true non-existence.

EIP-155 replay protection is effective: signing_hash_155 incorporates chain_id into the hash, preventing cross-chain replay of transactions. Pre-EIP-155 transactions (signing_hash_pre155) lack this protection and are assumed to be handled by legacy compatibility rules.

EIP-7702 authorization delegations in SetCodeTransaction are signed by the delegating EOA and bound to the transaction signature, preventing unauthorized code delegation. However, the delegated contract code is fully trusted to act in the EOA's interest.

Transient storage (EIP-1153) is correctly scoped to a single transaction: it is initialized empty, follows the snapshot/rollback lifecycle for internal calls, but is completely discarded after the transaction completes (INV-042).

The created_accounts tracking set correctly persists across rollbacks within a transaction (INV-013), ensuring get_storage_original returns U256(0) for newly created accounts regardless of intermediate reverts. This is critical for correct gas refund calculation (EIP-6780).

State root and storage root computations (state_root, storage_root) are only invoked outside active transactions (asserted by checking snapshots are empty), ensuring deterministic root computation without in-flight mutations.

The low-s normalization constraint on signatures (s <= SECP256K1N / 2, INV-002 of recover_sender) prevents transaction malleability, ensuring each valid transaction has exactly one canonical signature.

Contract creation initcode is bounded by MAX_INIT_CODE_SIZE (EIP-3860), and transaction gas is bounded by TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT of 16,777,216 (EIP-7825), preventing resource exhaustion attacks through oversized payloads.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could attempt to submit a transaction impersonating another EOA by forging the ECDSA signature (r, s, v/y_parity). If signature verification in recover_sender is bypassed or contains a bug, the wrong sender address would be recovered.
stride_analysis.mitigation: recover_sender validates that 0 < r < SECP256K1N and 0 < s <= SECP256K1N/2 before performing secp256k1 recovery. The sender address is derived deterministically from the recovered public key via keccak256. Low-s normalization prevents malleability-based spoofing.

stride_analysis.description: A transaction could be modified in transit (e.g., by a malicious P2P node or relayer) -- changing the recipient, amount, gas, or data fields. If the signing hash does not cover all critical fields, selective tampering could go undetected.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Each transaction type has a dedicated signing hash function (signing_hash_pre155, signing_hash_155, signing_hash_2930, signing_hash_1559, signing_hash_4844, signing_hash_7702) that includes all transaction fields in the signed payload. Any modification invalidates the signature. Typed transactions prefix the type byte into the hash.

stride_analysis.description: An EOA could deny having submitted a transaction after it has been executed and included in a block.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The ECDSA signature cryptographically binds the transaction to the sender's private key. The transaction hash (get_transaction_hash) provides a unique, verifiable identifier. On-chain inclusion in a block with a Merkle proof provides non-repudiable evidence.

stride_analysis.description: Transaction contents (recipient, amount, calldata) are visible to all network participants during propagation through the mempool before block inclusion, enabling front-running and sandwich attacks.
stride_analysis.mitigation: This is an inherent property of public blockchains. Mitigations are external to this specification (e.g., private mempools, commit-reveal schemes, encrypted transaction pools). The specification itself does not address mempool privacy.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could flood the transaction processor with invalid transactions (bad signatures, insufficient gas, nonce mismatches) to consume validation resources. Large initcode payloads or excessively high gas limits could amplify the cost of validation.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_transaction enforces early rejection: gas must cover max(intrinsic_gas, calldata_floor), nonce < U64.MAX_VALUE (EIP-2681), initcode <= MAX_INIT_CODE_SIZE, and gas <= TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT (16,777,216 per EIP-7825). Signature validation in recover_sender provides early rejection of malformed signatures.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could attempt to craft a transaction that, when decoded, is interpreted as a different transaction type than intended, potentially bypassing type-specific validation. For example, a specially crafted byte sequence could be misinterpreted between Legacy and typed transaction formats.
stride_analysis.mitigation: decode_transaction uses strict type checking: Legacy transactions are identified by being a non-Bytes RLP object, while typed transactions use unique, sequential prefix bytes (0x01-0x04). Unknown type prefixes raise TransactionTypeError. The type is bound to the signature via type-specific signing hashes.

stride_analysis.description: If the transaction processor passes an incorrectly validated transaction to the EVM (e.g., with wrong intrinsic gas calculation), the EVM would execute under false assumptions about available gas.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_transaction returns the exact (intrinsic_gas, calldata_floor) tuple. The EVM deducts intrinsic gas before execution. The EIP-7623 calldata floor check ensures transactions cannot underpay for calldata-heavy transactions.

stride_analysis.description: A SetCodeTransaction (EIP-7702) could allow an EOA to execute arbitrary contract code, potentially gaining capabilities not intended by the account holder if the authorization list is not properly validated.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The authorizations field is bound to the transaction signature (signing_hash_7702 includes authorizations). The intrinsic cost includes PER_EMPTY_ACCOUNT_COST per authorization to prevent free delegation. However, the delegated code has full access to the EOA's state, making this a high-trust delegation.

stride_analysis.description: If the EVM issues state mutations (set_storage, move_ether, set_account) without proper authorization checks, an attacker's contract could modify another account's storage or balance.
stride_analysis.mitigation: set_storage asserts that the target account exists in the main trie before allowing storage writes (INV-017). move_ether validates sufficient sender balance (INV-031). The EVM enforces that SSTORE/SLOAD operate only on the current contract's storage address.

stride_analysis.description: Excessive state mutations (creating many storage entries, many accounts) could bloat the state trie, increasing node storage requirements and slowing trie operations.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Gas costs for SSTORE, account creation, and storage operations make state bloat economically expensive. set_storage cleans up empty storage tries (INV-018/019). modify_state automatically destroys empty accounts (INV-028). Storage root computation is deferred to outside transactions (INV-020).

stride_analysis.description: The get_storage_original function (SG-024) reads from the outermost snapshot (state._snapshots[0]) to retrieve pre-transaction storage values. If snapshot boundaries are incorrect, it could leak state from a different transaction context.
stride_analysis.mitigation: get_storage_original accesses exactly snapshot[0] (the outermost/original state). For newly created accounts (in created_accounts set), it always returns U256(0) (INV-038). The snapshot index is deterministic and not controllable by contract code.

stride_analysis.description: If the trie implementation has bugs in trie_set or copy_trie, state could be silently corrupted. Incorrect copy_trie could create shallow copies where mutations leak between snapshot contexts.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification requires deep copies in begin_transaction (INV-003). The trie's Merkle root provides an integrity check: any tampering changes the root hash, which would be detected during consensus. Storage root and state root assertions enforce computation only when no snapshots are active (INV-020, INV-022).

stride_analysis.description: A smart contract could attempt to manipulate the call stack or exploit reentrancy to gain unauthorized access to another contract's storage or bypass access controls during nested calls.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The snapshot/rollback mechanism (begin_transaction/rollback_transaction) ensures that if an inner call reverts, all state changes are atomically undone (INV-006/007). Each storage operation is scoped to a specific address. Transient storage follows the same snapshot lifecycle, preventing cross-call leakage.

stride_analysis.description: A reentering contract could exploit the gap between balance checks and state updates in move_ether to extract more ether than authorized (classic reentrancy attack).
stride_analysis.mitigation: move_ether performs the balance sufficiency check (sender.balance >= amount) and immediately modifies state via modify_state. The checks-effects-interactions pattern is implicit in the sequential state machine (q_init -> q_reduce -> q_apply_sender -> q_increase -> q_final). Rollback_transaction provides atomic revert if any step fails.

stride_analysis.description: Signed transactions broadcast to the P2P network expose the sender's address, recipient, transfer amount, calldata, gas parameters, and nonce to all network observers before inclusion in a block.
stride_analysis.mitigation: No in-protocol mitigation exists in this specification layer. Transaction privacy requires external solutions (private transaction submission, encrypted mempools, or off-chain coordination).

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could broadcast a large volume of valid-looking but ultimately unexecutable transactions to flood the mempool and overwhelm node resources.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Transaction validation (validate_transaction) provides early, cheap rejection. The requirement for a valid signature ensures a minimum cost per submitted transaction. EIP-7825's TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT caps individual transaction resource claims.

stride_analysis.description: A validator/block builder can reorder transactions within a block to extract MEV (Miner/Maximal Extractable Value), front-running user transactions or inserting sandwich attacks.
stride_analysis.mitigation: No in-protocol mitigation at this specification layer. Transaction ordering is at the validator's discretion. External mitigations include proposer-builder separation (PBS), MEV-protection relays, and fair ordering protocols.

stride_analysis.description: A validator can censor specific transactions by refusing to include them in blocks, effectively denying service to specific users or contracts.
stride_analysis.mitigation: No direct mitigation at this specification layer. Censorship resistance relies on having a sufficiently decentralized and competitive validator set. Proposer-builder separation and inclusion lists are protocol-level mitigations outside this spec.

stride_analysis.description: Under EIP-7702 (SetCodeTransaction), an EOA delegates code execution to a contract. If the delegated contract is malicious or compromised, it gains full control over the EOA's account -- including balance, nonce, and storage -- effectively escalating from a passive code address to an active account controller.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The authorization is explicitly signed by the EOA and included in the transaction's signing hash. The EOA must intentionally choose the delegation target. However, once delegated, the contract code has unrestricted access to the EOA's state. Users must audit delegated contract code carefully.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could attempt to forge an EIP-7702 authorization, making it appear that an EOA has delegated code execution when it has not.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Authorizations are bound to the transaction signature via signing_hash_7702. The per-authorization cost (PER_EMPTY_ACCOUNT_COST) prevents free abuse. The authorization list is signed as part of the overall transaction, preventing injection of unauthorized delegations.

stride_analysis.description: Transient storage (TSTORE/TLOAD) could leak information between nested calls within the same transaction if the snapshot/rollback mechanism does not correctly isolate transient state.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Transient storage follows the same begin_transaction/commit_transaction/rollback_transaction lifecycle as persistent storage (SG-002, SG-003, SG-004). Snapshots include transient storage copies. On rollback, transient storage is restored from the snapshot (INV-007). Transient storage is completely cleared between transactions (INV-042).

stride_analysis.description: If snapshot copies in begin_transaction are shallow rather than deep, mutations to the live state would corrupt snapshot data, making rollback_transaction restore an incorrect state. This could enable an attacker to persist unauthorized state changes across a reverted sub-call.
stride_analysis.mitigation: begin_transaction creates copies via copy_trie for the main trie, per-address storage tries, and transient storage (INV-003). The specification explicitly requires that snapshots preserve deep copies such that mutations do not affect snapshots. commit_transaction and rollback_transaction each pop exactly one snapshot (INV-004, INV-006).

stride_analysis.description: Deeply nested transactions (many begin_transaction calls) could exhaust memory by creating an excessive number of trie deep copies, one per nesting level.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Gas costs for CALL/CREATE operations that trigger nested transactions limit nesting depth economically. Each nesting level costs gas, and the TX_MAX_GAS_LIMIT (EIP-7825) bounds total gas per transaction. The close_state function (SG-001) ensures resources are released after transaction processing.

stride_analysis.description: Pre-EIP-155 transactions (signing_hash_pre155) do not include chain_id in the signed payload, enabling cross-chain replay attacks where a transaction valid on one Ethereum-compatible chain can be replayed on another.
stride_analysis.mitigation: EIP-155 signing (signing_hash_155) appends (chain_id, 0, 0) to the signed fields, binding the transaction to a specific chain. All post-EIP-155 transaction types (2930, 1559, 4844, 7702) include chain_id in their signing hashes. Pre-155 transactions are maintained for backward compatibility but represent a known weaker security posture.
